event_blocks.summary: An electronic soundtrack with synth elements accompanies a space-themed montage showcasing the evolution of Samsung Galaxy S smartphones from the Galaxy S II to the S24, culminating in the text 'EVERY Galaxy S EVER'. A man in a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed on it introduces the "Samsung Galaxy S" as a popular smartphone name, questioning the origin of its name and revealing it comes from a wine enjoyed by Samsung executives, briefly showing a product page for 'Terlato Galaxy Red' wine. He asserts Samsung "will try anything," citing their extensive phone range. The video then displays a montage of various Samsung phones, including the Galaxy F34 5G, Galaxy Z Fold, Galaxy M series, Galaxy A series, and Galaxy Wonder, forming 'FAMILY OF WONDERS,' with a voice explaining the meaning of the letters F, Z, M, A, and W. Returning to the man, he introduces the flagship Samsung Galaxy S, stating 'S' stands for 'Super Smart,' and provides historical context: the first Samsung Galaxy S was released in 2010, challenging the iPhone and BlackBerry. He describes the 2010 Galaxy S as having a classic four capacitive buttons, Android 2.1, a 4-inch Samsung AMOLED display, and a 1500 mAh battery, noting its micro USB port and headphone jack. The man then introduces the "Samsung Galaxy S24" as a "supercomputer" with advanced features, stating he possesses every main Galaxy S variant from S1 to S24. He discusses the early Galaxy S's removable back and battery, noting its slowness but fastest graphics processing thanks to a Samsung-made chip, capable of 720p video and expandable storage via micro SD. He highlights the "modern shape" of later models with no camera bump and points out a small bump over the rear speaker to prevent blockage. The man identifies early variants like the 'Galaxy S Stratosphere,' 'Galaxy S Vibrant,' and 'Galaxy S Epic 4G,' noting Samsung's strategy of creating different versions for each US carrier. The narrative progresses to the 2011 Samsung Galaxy S2, described as bigger and sleeker with significant year-over-year spec increases, including a 1.2 GHz dual-core chip, a 4.3-inch screen, an 8-megapixel camera shooting 1080p, and doubled RAM to one gigabyte.

Terlato Galaxy Red Wine Product Page.description: A digital product page for 'Terlato Galaxy Red' wine, showing a dark bottle with 'GALAXY' branding, a 4.2-star rating, price ($94.56), and taste profile details including bold, tannic, sweet, and acidic characteristics, with a description of aromas and flavor notes, which is presented visually as the speaker explains its surprising connection to the Samsung Galaxy S naming convention.

Man in Black Shirt.description: A man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray letters, seated in a modern room with a large monitor displaying a landscape in the background. He speaks directly to the camera with an informative tone, using expressive hand gestures while holding and comparing smartphones. He discusses Samsung's strategy of creating carrier-specific versions to bypass exclusives, introduces the Galaxy S2 as the 'first sequel,' and details its significant upgrades over the original Galaxy S, including processor speed, screen size, camera resolution, and RAM.

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold.description: A silver Samsung foldable smartphone with a sleek design and a large display showing a purple abstract pattern, accompanied by a large 'Z' beside it. The accompanying audio clarifies that 'Z' stands for 'zero'.

Samsung Galaxy F34 5G.description: A black Samsung smartphone with a vibrant screen displaying '5G F34' in bold white text, shown against a neutral background with a large 'F' appearing beside it. The accompanying audio clarifies that 'F' stands for 'fun'.

Samsung Galaxy M Series.description: A white Samsung smartphone with a colorful abstract wallpaper on the screen, shown with a large 'M' beside it. The accompanying audio clarifies that 'M' actually stands for 'millennial', not mid-range.

Samsung Galaxy Wonder.description: A black Samsung smartphone with a classic interface and a large 'W' beside it, shown with a vintage design aesthetic. The accompanying audio clarifies that 'W' stands for 'Wonder'.

Samsung Galaxy A Series.description: A purple Samsung smartphone with a dynamic abstract wallpaper, displayed with a large 'A' beside it. The accompanying audio clarifies that 'A' stands for 'Alpha' and notes it's one of their most popular affordable smartphones.

Purple Samsung Smartphone.description: A modern purple Samsung smartphone held in a hand, showcasing its slim profile and vibrant screen with app icons, displayed in a real-world setting with a blurred background.

Black Samsung Smartphone (Classic Interf.description: A black Samsung smartphone with a textured black casing and a single camera lens with a flash on the back. It displays a classic Android interface with app icons like Calculator, Camera, and Messaging, and the time '5:36 PM', and is shown navigating Amazon.com and launching the Angry Birds game. The man describes it as having a removable back for battery swapping, a single 5-megapixel camera capable of 720p video, 2-4GB of internal storage, and a microSD card expandable storage slot, noting its performance is 'slow' but not 'laggy' for tasks like web browsing or opening the camera, despite having 'the fastest graphics processing' at its launch.

S24 Label.description: A teal-colored text overlay reading 'S24' that appears next to the newer white Samsung smartphone, which the speaker refers to as 'the S24' in his comparison.

White Samsung Smartphone (Modern).description: A modern white Samsung smartphone with a triple-camera module on the back, held alongside a purple model. The speaker identifies it as the 'Samsung Galaxy S24' and describes it as an 'ultra-refined, tightly packed supercomputer' with 'high resolution cameras and displays, powerful processors, AI features,' and 'a ton of optimizations.'

S1 Label.description: A teal-colored text overlay reading 'S1' that appears next to the 2010 Galaxy S to visually denote its model generation, aligning with the speaker's discussion of it being the 'first one'.

2010 Galaxy S.description: A black Samsung Galaxy S smartphone from 2010, shown in various close-ups on a desk, displaying its classic Android interface, physical buttons, and internal components when the back cover is removed. It features a Verizon carrier label, a 3.5mm headphone jack, and a removable battery. It is implicitly referred to by the speaker as the 'first Galaxy S' when comparing it to its successor.

2011 Galaxy S2.description: A white Samsung Galaxy S2 smartphone from 2011, shown in multiple angles on a desk, displaying its home screen with app icons and time. The speaker describes it as 'notably bigger and notably sleeker' than the first Galaxy S. It features a sleek design with a 4.3-inch screen (up from 4-inch), an 8.0 MP camera on the back (up from 5 MP 720p, now shooting 1080p), and a removable battery. Internally, it boasts a 1.2 GHz dual-core chip (up from 1 GHz), more than doubling processing power, and doubled RAM up to a gigabyte. The phone is shown standing upright and leaning against a decorative wire basket with a glowing light inside.

raw_segments: An energetic, electronic soundtrack with rhythmic beats and synth elements accompanies a dynamic space-themed montage showcasing the evolution of Samsung Galaxy S smartphones from the Galaxy S II to the S24, displaying each model against a starry backdrop with glowing neon years and floating Android mascots, culminating in the vibrant teal text 'EVERY Galaxy S EVER'. The scene then cuts to a man, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed on it, who begins speaking directly to the camera in a clear, engaging voice. He introduces the "Samsung Galaxy S" as one of the most popular smartphone names, noting its longevity and posing a question about the origin of the "Galaxy S" name, suggesting common assumptions are incorrect, all while using expressive hand gestures in a modern room with a scenic monitor. As he explains that "Galaxy" apparently comes from a wine "often enjoyed by high-level Samsung executives," the video briefly shifts to a product page for 'Terlato Galaxy Red' wine, displaying its dark bottle, a 4.2-star rating, price ($94.56), and taste profile details like bold, tannic, sweet, and acidic characteristics. The scene returns to the man, who, with animated gestures including pointing and framing motions, continues his commentary, asserting that "Samsung will try anything," a characteristic he's observed from reviewing their phones and further confirmed by researching for this video, highlighting their extensive range of phones across every imaginable price point.

The video opens with a dynamic montage of various Samsung smartphones, including the Galaxy Express, Galaxy F34 5G, Galaxy Z Fold, Galaxy M series, Galaxy A series, and Galaxy Wonder. Each phone is displayed with a large letter (F, Z, M, A, W) appearing beside it, collectively forming the phrase 'FAMILY OF WONDERS.' A male voice, later identified as the Man in Black Shirt, begins by discussing features and spec sheets, then challenges viewers to guess what the letters stand for. He reveals that 'F' in the Galaxy F series stands for 'fun,' 'Z' in the Galaxy Z series stands for 'zero,' and 'M' in the Galaxy M series, which one might think is for mid-range, actually stands for 'millennial.' He continues, explaining that 'A' in the popular, affordable Galaxy A series stands for 'Alpha,' and 'W' in the Galaxy Wonder stands for 'Wonder.' He also briefly mentions the Galaxy R for 'Royal,' noting that the list goes on. The sequence transitions to a close-up of a hand holding a sleek purple Samsung smartphone, showcasing its vibrant screen. The scene then cuts to the Man in Black Shirt, a man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt, seated in a modern, well-lit room with a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape. He speaks directly to the camera with an enthusiastic and informative tone, using expressive hand gestures. He introduces the flagship Samsung Galaxy S, and as the text 'SUPER SMART.' appears on screen, he emphatically states that 'S' stands for 'Super Smart.' He then provides historical context, explaining that the first-ever Samsung Galaxy S was released in 2010, three years after the first iPhone, at a time when the iPhone 4 was about to launch, the Nexus One had just been unveiled, and BlackBerry was 'quaking in their boots,' signifying a complete reshuffling of the entire smartphone industry.

The segment opens with the Man in Black Shirt, wearing a 'MATTE BLACK' shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a modern, well-lit room, gesturing expressively. He introduces the topic, noting that the second most popular phone by volume "looks like this." A close-up then reveals a black phone with a classic interface, displaying '01:24' and app icons, placed on a desk. The man identifies this as a "Nokia 3720 Classic," describing it as a "pretty basic phone" with a "big touchscreen on the front, some capacitive buttons, a plastic removable back, a single camera." He then announces that "Samsung launches the first ever Galaxy S, super smart into the world with over two dozen variations." The black phone is shown from different angles, highlighting its slim profile and a screen displaying product pages for 'MATTE BLACK EVERYTHING T-SHIRT' and 'BLACK CORE LOGO T-SHIRT'. The scene transitions to a hand holding two modern Samsung smartphones, one purple and one white, showcasing their rear camera modules. The Man in Black Shirt reappears, now holding both phones, comparing them. He introduces the "Samsung Galaxy S24" as an "ultra-refined, tightly packed supercomputer" with "high resolution cameras and displays, powerful processors, AI features," and "a ton of optimizations." He states he has "every single Samsung Galaxy S that has ever been released," clarifying it's "every main Galaxy S variant from the S1 to the S24, everything in between." Text overlays appear, labeling the older phone as 'S1' and the newer one as 'S24', as the man continues his comparison, gesturing between the two devices, promising to review them all.

The video opens with the Man in Black Shirt, who has short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black 'MATTE BLACK' shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, informative voice. He explains his intent to "go back and go through" the story of smartphone evolution, from "where it started" to "where we are today," promising "graphs at the end" that are "super fun." He holds and compares two smartphones, emphasizing design evolution, before introducing the "Samsung Galaxy S," explicitly stating, "This is the first one. Summer 2010." The scene transitions to a close-up of the black 2010 Galaxy S, lying on a desk, with a teal 'S1' label appearing beside it. The speaker notes its "clearly look[ing] old with the classic four capacitive buttons at the bottom, Android 2.1, and it's a micro USB port at the top next to the headphone jack." He highlights Samsung's early differentiation, praising its "reasonable display" as a "4-inch screen" and a "Samsung made AMOLED display," calling it "a huge deal." He explains how AMOLED technology, where "the pixels didn't have to light up on the OLED," was a "new concept back then" that helped conserve battery life, especially with black wallpapers, given its "only a 1500 mAh battery." The phone's screen displays a classic Android interface with app icons like Phone, Contacts, Messaging, and The Weather Channel, along with a Verizon carrier label. The camera pans around the device, highlighting its physical buttons, side ports, and Samsung branding. A hand interacts with the phone, swiping across the screen to reveal a colorful wallpaper featuring the Android mascot and a gingerbread man. The video concludes with the phone’s back cover removed, revealing the internal battery and SIM card slot, emphasizing its hardware components.

The video opens with the Man in Black Shirt, a man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray letters, seated in a modern, well-lit room. He speaks directly to the camera, holding a black Samsung smartphone, and gestures expressively. He discusses the phone's historical feature of a removable back and battery, noting, "this was back in the day, when you could just pop the back off any phone and just take the battery out, swap it out at any moment, have two batteries if you want to, swap them in and out real quick while the others are charging." He adds, "We will witness the death of this feature during this video." He then shifts to discussing the phone's current performance, acknowledging it "feels slow, obviously," but clarifies it's "not really in the laggy or unresponsive way, it's just slow." He mentions "a lot of solid waiting for anything to happen," especially with "heavier stuff like the web browser or opening the camera," advising to "slow down a little bit, give it a second." He highlights that the phone "launched with what at the time was the fastest graphics processing of any smartphone, thanks to a Samsung made chip inside." The man demonstrates the phone's functionality, showing its classic Android home screen with app icons like Calculator, Camera, and Messaging, and the time '5:36 PM'. He navigates to Amazon.com in the browser, scrolling through the site, and then launches the Angry Birds game, which displays its colorful title screen and 'LOADING...' text. He continues, noting the phone "launched with a single 5-megapixel camera on the back, which was capable of shooting actually 720p video right out the box." He explains it "asks for a memory card to do a lot of things like shooting videos because it only comes with two or four gigs of storage out the box," but confirms "there's a micro SD card expandable storage slot in the back of the phone." A close-up shot reveals the phone’s textured black casing and single camera lens with a flash, propped against a potted plant, before the man returns to frame, continuing his informative and engaging commentary.

The segment opens in a modern, well-lit room where a man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray letters, speaks directly to the camera. He holds a black Samsung smartphone, gesturing expressively as he discusses its features, noting its capacity of "up to 32 gigs right off the bat." He describes the phone's "modern shape" with "flat sides and a flat back," and points out that "there is no camera bump." The background features a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape, a white desk with a keyboard, a silver helmet, and a pink speaker. As the man explains, the camera cuts to close-up shots of the phone, highlighting its glossy black back with a textured pattern, 'GALAXY S' branding, a single rear camera with a flash, and curved metallic edges. He elaborates on a "small bump in the plastic actually over the rear-facing speaker," explaining its purpose: "without that bump, it would block the speaker," ensuring "you can always hear your sound." The phone is shown standing upright on a desk, emphasizing its slim profile and physical buttons. The man continues, holding the phone in one hand and gesturing with the other, remarking on Samsung's tendency to "just try anything," which was true "since the first one." He states that "this first ever Galaxy S launched with... over a dozen variants." On-screen text overlays appear in teal font, identifying the phone as 'Galaxy S Stratosphere', 'Galaxy S Vibrant', and 'Galaxy S Epic 4G'. He specifically identifies one variant as the "Samsung Galaxy S Stratosphere," noting it has "the little Verizon stamp on its forehead." He then mentions the "Samsung Galaxy S Vibrant" as the T-Mobile variant and the "Samsung Galaxy S Epic 4G." The bright, even lighting consistently keeps the focus on the speaker and the device.

The video opens with a detailed technical specification table for various Samsung Galaxy S models in North America, highlighting differences across carriers, models, dimensions, and features. A male voice explains that Samsung created slightly different versions of the phone for every carrier in America, with varying designs and features, to navigate carrier exclusives. The scene then transitions to a man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed on it, seated in a modern, well-lit room with a large monitor displaying a landscape. He holds a black Samsung Galaxy S smartphone, gesturing expressively as he speaks directly to the camera, noting that this strategy was the beginning of a "whole empire." The video shifts to a montage showcasing the evolution of Samsung Galaxy S models, starting with the black 2010 Galaxy S displayed on a desk with a '2010 GALAXY S' text overlay, showing its classic Android interface and physical buttons. The man's voice then introduces the "first sequel," the 2011 Samsung Galaxy S2. This white model, with a '2011 GALAXY S2' overlay, is shown on a desk next to a keyboard and mouse. The man describes it as "notably bigger and notably sleeker" than the first Galaxy S, highlighting the "crazy" year-over-year percentage increases in specifications. The Galaxy S2 is shown from multiple angles, including a close-up of its back with the camera and 'GALAXY S II' branding, and its internal components revealing the battery and SIM card slot. The man details its upgrades: from a 1 GHz chip to a 1.2 GHz dual-core chip, more than doubling processing power; from a 4-inch screen to a 4.3-inch screen; from a 5-megapixel 720p camera to an 8-megapixel camera on the back shooting 1080p; and doubled RAM up to a gigabyte. The phone is also shown standing upright on a desk, displaying its home screen with app icons and time, and leaning against a decorative wire basket with a glowing light inside, emphasizing the design, features, and internal hardware of these early Galaxy S models and their significance in smartphone evolution.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a detailed examination of the 2011 Samsung Galaxy S II, highlighting its internal components like the SIM card slot and battery, and noting its 1650 mAh battery, 'octupled' storage from 2GB to 16GB, and an 8.5-millimeter slim profile with NFC. The commentator discusses its price increase from $399 to $549 and its 'relative success' of 3 million units sold in two months. The focus then shifts to the 2012 Galaxy S3, identified as the first Samsung Galaxy S the commentator owned, characterized by its 4.8-inch screen, which was considered large at the time, and a design inspired by nature with water droplet sounds. The transition from four to three capacitive buttons, with the removal of the search button, is noted. The 'S Voice' assistant is introduced as a response to Siri, though the commentator candidly admits it 'wasn't good,' visually demonstrated by repeated server errors. The S3's 'super sleek design' and its reception as a potential 'iPhone killer' are mentioned, alongside the introduction of the smaller Galaxy S3 Mini due to the S3's large screen. The segment concludes with the 2013 Galaxy S4, described as the 'defining one' in its product generation, with the commentator holding a black version of the phone against a backdrop of a modern room with a large monitor, desk, robot figurine, and yellow mug.

Man in Black Shirt.description: A man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray letters, seated in a modern room. He speaks directly to the camera in a clear, conversational tone, describing the Samsung Galaxy S3's design, OS, features (slim, fast, OLED, S Voice), market perception as an 'iPhone killer,' and screen size (4.8-inch). He also discusses the S3 Mini (4-inch) and the significance of the Samsung Galaxy S4 as a 'defining' fourth-generation product, using expressive hand gestures while holding and comparing two smartphones.

2012 Galaxy S3.description: A white Samsung Galaxy S3 smartphone from 2012, described by the speaker as 'shaped like a thin pebble' and 'inspired by nature.' It features 'Verizon 4G' branding on the back and a dandelion wallpaper on the lock screen, which the speaker notes had an OS with 'water droplet sounds everywhere' and an interactive 'swipe to unlock' feature that evolved to 'dragging your finger through this water.' The phone is highlighted for being 'super slim and fast' with a 'huge OLED and S Voice,' and was 'touted like the iPhone killer.' Its '4.8-inch screen' is mentioned as being 'kinda giant,' leading to the S3 Mini. It is shown displaying '9:32 AM' and '11:06 AM' at different points.

2011 Galaxy S2.description: A white Samsung Galaxy S2 smartphone from 2011, shown in multiple angles on a desk, displaying its home screen with app icons and time. It features a sleek design with a 4.3-inch screen, an 8.0 MP camera on the back, and a removable battery. The Man in Black Shirt describes its 1650 mAh battery, storage increasing from 2GB to 16GB, its thinness (8.5mm), the addition of NFC, and its launch price of $399-$549, selling 3 million units in two months. He notes it was on his radar but he never owned one.

2013 Galaxy S4.description: A dark blue Samsung Galaxy S4 smartphone from 2013, shown in a flat-lay composition. The speaker introduces it as the 'Samsung Galaxy S4' released 'the next year' after the S3, marking 'the beginning of a lot of things' and considered a 'defining' fourth-generation product. The text '2013 GALAXY S4' is displayed next to it.

Black Samsung Smartphone (Classic Interf.description: A black Samsung smartphone with a classic Android interface, shown being held by the Man in Black Shirt. It displays the time '9:51 AM' on its screen and is used for comparison with the white Galaxy S3. The phone has a sleek design with a single rear camera and flash.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up view of the internal components of a white Samsung Galaxy S II, revealing its SIM card slot, battery compartment, visible screws, and a '29kh' marking on the circuitry. A man in a black shirt, the commentator, begins speaking, detailing the phone's 1650 mAh battery and how its storage 'octupled' from 2GB to 16GB. He then holds the white Galaxy S II, rotating it to showcase its slim profile, back design, camera, and 'Sprint' branding, emphasizing that the phone 'got thinner' and added NFC, making it an 8.5-millimeter device—one of the thinnest in the world at the time. The phone is then seen resting on a desk next to a colorful mechanical keyboard. The commentator continues, mentioning the S II's price increase from $399 to $549, its 'relative success' with 3 million units sold in two months, and his personal experience of seeing it on his radar but never owning one. The scene transitions to a top-down view of the phone on a white surface, with the text '2012 GALAXY S3' appearing, signaling a shift. The Galaxy S3 is then shown upright on a shelf, displaying a cheerful orange Android mascot. The commentator identifies this as the 'Galaxy S3 in 2012,' the first Samsung Galaxy S he actually owned, expressing his fondness for it. A hand picks up the white Galaxy S III, revealing its 'Verizon 4G' branding, and places it beside a retro-style checkered camera for size comparison. The man in the black shirt reappears, holding the Galaxy S III, and with animated gestures, discusses its 'very real' jump in size to a 4.8-inch phone, which he notes was 'pushing the limits' of what was considered an appropriate phone size at the time.

The minute opens with the Man in Black Shirt, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt, seated in a modern, well-lit room, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He reviews a white Samsung smartphone, explaining that its 4.65-inch screen was a popular 'big phone' size at the time, and how anything near 5 inches felt 'weird, like a tablet size,' before Samsung's flagship pushed 4.8 inches. He details the phone's design, noting the transition from four to three capacitive buttons (home, back, menu) at the front, with the search button removed. The scene transitions to close-up shots of a white Samsung Galaxy S3 from 2012, first upright on a shelf displaying the time '4:42 PM' and a blue water-themed wallpaper, then zooming in on its home screen with app icons. The Man in Black Shirt introduces the phone's voice assistant, 'S Voice,' explaining it was a direct response to Siri on the iPhone and that 'S' stood for Samsung, also used in 'S Notes.' He candidly states that 'S Voice wasn't good,' a point visually reinforced as the phone is shown lying flat on a peach-colored surface, its screen repeatedly displaying the error message 'Sorry, something went wrong with the server. Please try again' after commands like 'Set timer for 1 minute' and 'Navigate to Cambridge, MA.' He notes that other voice assistants at the time also struggled with simple tasks like 'random facts' or 'weather.' The video cuts back to the Man in Black Shirt, who continues his commentary while holding the phone, before concluding with a close-up of the back of the white Galaxy S3 on a yellow surface, clearly showing the 'Verizon 4G' logo, 'GALAXY S III' branding, and its camera lens and flash, as the speaker emphasizes the phone's 'super sleek design.'

The video opens with a close-up of a white Samsung Galaxy S3 smartphone, its back displaying 'Verizon 4G' and 'GALAXY S III' branding, which the speaker describes as being "shaped like a thin pebble" and "inspired by nature." The camera zooms in on its rear camera and flash before a hand unlocks the phone, revealing a lock screen with a dandelion wallpaper, the time '9:32 AM', and a 'Connect your charger' prompt. The speaker notes that the S3's "whole OS leaned into that," featuring "a ton of water droplet sounds everywhere," and its lock screen interaction evolved from a simple swipe to "dragging your finger through this water." After swiping to the home screen with app icons and a 'Your Personal Magazine' widget, the Man in Black Shirt, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt, holds the white Galaxy S3, speaking directly to the camera. He recounts his personal experience "using this phone and reviewing it" back in college, expressing his fondness for it and noting its positive reception. He highlights its features, such as being "super slim and fast" with a "huge OLED and S Voice," which led it to be "touted like the iPhone killer." He then holds up a second, black Samsung smartphone, comparing the two devices, and explains that the S3's "4.8-inch screen" was considered "kinda giant," prompting the release of a "4-inch display, Galaxy S3 Mini." A top-down view shows the white S3 displaying '11:06 AM' next to the black phone showing '9:51 AM'. The scene transitions to a flat-lay shot of a dark blue Samsung Galaxy S4 from 2013, surrounded by various objects, with the text '2013 GALAXY S4' displayed. The speaker announces, "the next year we got Samsung Galaxy S4, which was the beginning of a lot of things," and reflects on how the fourth generation of a product like this is often "the defining one." The Man in Black Shirt reappears, holding the black phone, continuing his commentary with expressive hand gestures, all set against a consistent background of a modern room with a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape, a white desk, a robot figurine, and a yellow mug.

event_blocks.summary: A Man in Black Shirt, characterized by short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black 'MATTE BLACK' shirt, addresses the camera in a modern, well-lit room, emphasizing the increasing capabilities of "little pocket computers." He highlights "gigantic leaps on paper with specs," specifically showcasing a black 2010 Samsung Galaxy S smartphone. The Man in Black Shirt details its screen evolution from a 4.8-inch 720p display to a 5-inch 1080p screen, noting its pioneering status among phones with such a display during the advent of new retina displays. Close-ups reveal the phone's classic Android interface displaying '9:51 AM' and a Verizon carrier label, later transitioning to '12:02 PM' with a blue gradient wallpaper when placed upright on a patterned desk beside a white keyboard and notebook. He elaborates on processor upgrades from 1.4 to 1.9 GHz dual-core, RAM doubling from 1 to 2 gigabytes, and the rear camera enhancement from 8 to 13 megapixels, enabling "pretty sick" 1080p video recording and playback. While holding the 2010 Galaxy S and comparing it with another device, he discusses the battery upgrade from 2000 mAh to 2600 mAh, stressing its "still removable" nature and calling the "30% increase pretty crazy." He reiterates the persistent industry pursuit of "one day of battery life," a goal present "since this long ago." Throughout the explanation, a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape, a window with blinds, and desk accessories like a small robot figurine and a yellow mug form the consistent background.

Man in Black Shirt.description: A man with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray letters, seated in a modern room with a large monitor displaying a landscape in the background. He uses expressive hand gestures while speaking directly to the camera, providing detailed technical specifications and historical context for smartphone evolution, specifically discussing screen size, resolution, processor speed, RAM, camera megapixels, 1080p video capability, and battery capacity/removability, expressing approval with "Pretty sick." He holds and compares two smartphones.

2010 Galaxy S.description: A black Samsung Galaxy S smartphone from 2010, which the Man in Black Shirt describes as evolving from a 4.8-inch 720p screen to a 5-inch 1080p screen, with processor upgrades from 1.4 to 1.9 GHz dual-core, RAM doubling from 1 to 2 GB, and its single back camera improving from 8 to 13 megapixels, enabling 1080p video recording and playback. He also notes its battery capacity increasing from 2000 mAh to 2600 mAh, emphasizing it's "still removable." Visually, it's shown in various close-ups on a desk, displaying its classic Android interface, physical buttons, and internal components when the back cover is removed. It features a Verizon carrier label, a 3.5mm headphone jack, and a removable battery.

raw_segments: The Man in Black Shirt, with short curly hair and a beard, wearing a black 'MATTE BLACK' shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a modern, well-lit room, gesturing expressively as he introduces how "these little pocket computers are getting more and more capable." He details the "gigantic leaps on paper with specs," showcasing a black 2010 Samsung Galaxy S smartphone. He explains its evolution from a 4.8-inch 720p screen to a 5-inch 1080p screen, noting it was "one of the first phones" with such a display in the era of new retina displays. Close-ups of the phone show its classic Android interface, displaying '9:51 AM' with a Verizon carrier label. The scene shifts to the phone placed upright on a patterned desk next to a white keyboard and notebook, now showing '12:02 PM' and a blue gradient wallpaper. The man continues, describing processor upgrades from 1.4 to 1.9 GHz dual-core, RAM doubling from 1 to 2 gigabytes, and the single back camera improving from 8 to 13 megapixels, enabling 1080p video recording and playback, which he calls "pretty sick." He then holds the 2010 Galaxy S, comparing it with another device, likely a newer model, as he discusses the battery upgrade from 2000 mAh to 2600 mAh, emphasizing it's "still removable" and that a "30% increase is pretty crazy." He highlights the ongoing industry goal of "one day of battery life," a theme "since this long ago." The background consistently features a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape, a window with blinds, and various desk accessories including a small robot figurine and a yellow mug.

event_blocks.summary: A Man in Black Shirt, with short curly hair and a beard, seated in a modern, well-lit room, holds a black Samsung smartphone and discusses its increasing power and features, emphasizing the importance of a full day's battery life. He explains that the phone was part of Samsung's experimental phase, incorporating numerous features, some of which he finds intriguing, particularly the AI capabilities. The camera then focuses on the smartphone's screen, displaying the 'Smart screen' settings menu with options like 'Smart stay,' 'Smart rotation,' and 'Smart pause.' event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Shirt, a bearded individual in a black 'MATTE BLACK' shirt, seated in a modern, well-lit room, speaks directly to the camera while gesturing expressively. He initially demonstrates a black Samsung smartphone with a classic Android interface, highlighting its '1440p AMOLED display,' 'optical image stabilization,' and '64-bit processing,' noting its positive reception for build quality and features, but criticizing 'TouchWiz' as a drawback. The scene then shifts to a silver HTC smartphone held by a Person in Red Columbia Shirt, with the speaker mentioning other Android options like the Nexus 6P and 5X before identifying the device as the 'HTC 1 M9.' Returning to the Man in Black Shirt, he discusses how Android devices, including HTC and LG G4, came with proprietary software, contrasting Samsung's improved but still present 'TouchWiz' skin, which offered extra features but could slow performance and delay updates. He expresses a preference for 'stock Android' due to faster updates and a cleaner experience, while acknowledging TouchWiz's features despite its 'colorful cartoony look.' He then presents the Samsung Galaxy S6 and S6 Edge, explaining Samsung's response to the bezel war with the S6 Edge's innovative curved display that appeared to 'bleed right over the edges.' A montage showcases a gold Samsung Galaxy S6 rotating, highlighting its design. The Man in Black Shirt further discusses the Galaxy Note's software tickers leading to the S series, with the S6 Edge Plus achieving a 76% screen-to-body ratio. He then introduces the 2016 Galaxy S7 as an incremental update, noting the return of expandable storage via a 'clever' micro SD card integration. He then details the battery capacities of the S7 Edge, S7 Edge Plus, and S7 Active, recalling the 'Galaxy Note 7' incident and its 3500 mAh battery issues, explaining the 'exact issue' was a 'design issue using a thin separator between two electrodes in a battery,' which was easily damaged, leading to 'wild reactions,' and calling it the 'greatest PR disaster in the history of smartphones.' The video then shows a news article about the Note 7 ban on planes and a damaged battery. The Man in Black Shirt compares the Note 7 issues to Samsung's ability to 'weather that storm,' then contrasts a black Samsung smartphone with a modern white one featuring a triple-camera module, before introducing the 'Samsung Galaxy S8' as a favorite, highlighting its 'fully curved edge displays' and refined 'edge screen apps feature.' Close-ups of the S8's screen display a purple gradient wallpaper. He then discusses a 'subtle' yet 'useful' pressure-sensitive layer beneath the display around the home button on a black Samsung smartphone, comparing it to Apple's '3D Touch,' and notes the phone's 'slightly taller, narrower 18 and a half by 9 aspect ratio,' the shift to 'all on-screen buttons,' and the first-time switch to 'USBC,' calling it a 'sick, easy win,' before introducing the 'Dex' feature for connecting to a monitor.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern, well-lit room with a large monitor displaying a landscape, the Man in Black Shirt, identifiable by his short curly hair, beard, and a black long-sleeve shirt with 'MATTE BLACK' printed in gray, critically analyzes Samsung's smartphone strategies. Initially, he explains that Samsung's decision to use both Qualcomm Snapdragon and their own Exynos chips for the Galaxy S8 was a strategic move for efficiency, but it "failed spectacularly" as Snapdragon versions consistently outperformed, leading buyers to actively seek them out, even with tutorials to identify and avoid the Exynos variant. This split strategy, despite the phone justifying a price increase to $750, was deemed a "big backfire." He then introduces the "Galaxy S9," showcasing a purple Samsung Galaxy S9 in a stylized product shot, praising its "nice colors" and "slightly improved processor," and noting the fingerprint sensor's relocation below the camera. The video highlights the S9's "super cool" dual aperture camera, capable of switching between f/1.5 and f/2.4 for optimal light capture, visually demonstrated by a montage of impressive nighttime photos. The Man expresses his fondness for this feature, lamenting its absence in later models and urging Samsung to "Bring it back." He concludes by comparing the purple S9 with a black Samsung smartphone displaying a classic interface with the time '9:42' and weather, expressing a desire to "bring back any old phone design" and modernize it with new specifications.

He speaks directly to the camera, using expressive hand gestures, critically describing the phone's appearance as "blank, boring, soulless," and questioning, "What happened?" He continues, stating it "feels like this is a complete blank phone." The camera then cuts to a close-up of a black Samsung Galaxy S20 standing upright on a geometric-patterned desk, with a large monitor displaying a swirling blue and purple abstract wallpaper behind it. The Man in Black Shirt's voice continues, comparing the S20 to previous models, remarking, "It looks like they took a year off with the S20, like this might be the most generic phone of all time." A tight shot highlights the phone’s triple-lens rear camera module. He acknowledges that other phones have looked similar but emphasizes that "this is Samsung's flagship for years," concluding that "in hindsight, this one is pretty dull." The scene shifts back to the Man in Black Shirt holding the phone up, showing its sleek black back, as he points out it's the "first one to not say the model number on the back at all with the text. It's just blank." The phone is then placed upright on the desk, screen facing forward, displaying a vibrant home screen with a colorful abstract wallpaper, weather information (36°), and app icons. The camera pans around the phone, showing it lying flat on the desk with a green circular animation on the screen, then focuses on its side profile, emphasizing the slim design and volume buttons. The speaker notes that it's the "first ever Galaxy S... to move the volume rocker to the right side. That's a choice." He also mentions it's the "first Galaxy S to follow in the footsteps of the iPhone... and removing the headphone jack. Also a choice." The video returns to the Man in Black Shirt, who continues to interact with the phone, pointing at its screen and discussing its features, finally praising the "120 Hz display" and the "smaller hole punch." The final shot shows the phone lying on the desk next to a keyboard and trackpad, with the screen displaying the same colorful home screen, reinforcing its modern design and interface.

event_blocks.summary: A black Samsung smartphone, identified as potentially a Galaxy S24, is shown with its camera interface displaying '8K 24' and '9:16' settings, highlighting its 8K video recording capability. He compares a black Samsung smartphone, possibly a Galaxy S10, with another device, then presents a blue Samsung smartphone, likely the Galaxy S20, showcasing its triple-camera module. The speaker clarifies that the S20 was a regular-sized, $1000 phone, distinct from the 'Ultra' models which competed with the Galaxy Note, a successful high-end phone known for its stylus. He notes that the S20 was initially overlooked until the release of the 'Fan Edition' (FE) later in the year, which featured reduced specifications such as 1080p recording. The video concludes with a static shot of the blue Galaxy S20 on a windowsill, its back facing the camera, bathed in natural light alongside a blue glass bottle and magazines.

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Shirt, initially praising the 2020 Galaxy S20 as his "phone of the year" and a "great deal," reveals a "not so fun fact": its battery has begun swelling and bulging, pushing the back off the chassis. He then critiques Samsung's trend of battery swelling, noting it's the "only brand" to have multiple such incidents. Transitioning to the 2021 Galaxy S21, he highlights its "iconic" purple and gold colorway, "current design language," and "unmistakable" triple camera, while criticizing its "base model" design as "extremely boring" and "playing it safe." He points out the S21's "back to flat displays," reduced 1080p resolution, lack of expandable storage, and absence of a charger in the box. Finally, discussing the 2022 Galaxy S22, he describes it as following a similar track with "slightly more boring colors" and "slightly flatter edges," emphasizing its "perfectly thin bezels" and high screen-to-body ratio, arguing that flat displays are now preferable to curved ones for durability and aesthetics. He notes the S22 models are slightly smaller than their predecessors, with screen sizes reduced from 6.2 and 6.7 inches to 6 inches.

The video opens with a top-down shot of a purple Samsung Galaxy S21 lying on a white surface, surrounded by minimalist objects, with '2021 GALAXY S21' text. This transitions to a black Samsung Galaxy S22 on a similar surface with '2022 GALAXY S22' and stationery. He highlights its "perfectly thin bezels all the way around," noting that the screen-to-body ratio is "well into the 90s" and the "bezels effectively gone." He continues, stating that it "makes perfect sense to go back to the perfectly flat displays" because curved displays were a "crutch" to make phones "appear to have a larger screen-to-body ratio than it actually did." He asserts that with current technology, there's no need to curve the display, as it "just looks sick, and is flatter, and is more durable." Product shots of the Galaxy S22 reappear, showcasing its back with the 'SAMSUNG' logo and triple-camera module, placed upright on a wooden desk with warm lighting, and in dark blue and silver variants. The speaker concludes by mentioning that the "most notable change on the outside" is that "these phones are now slightly smaller than the previous year," having gone from "6.2 and 6.7 inches, to 6."

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Shirt, in a well-lit modern room, discusses the evolution of Samsung Galaxy smartphones, starting with the S23, highlighting its unified Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy chip, eliminating the Exynos version and ensuring consistent high performance across all models. He contrasts the S23's design, noting the removal of the camera bump for a more generic appeal and slightly larger batteries, while acknowledging the shared components and aspect ratios with previous generations. The narrative then shifts to the S24, characterized by its even more conservative design and colors, but enhanced with substantial AI features like 'live translation' and 'Circle to Search,' powered by the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 chip, and generative photo editing capabilities. He demonstrates these features, including a stylus interacting with a smartphone for drawing and a call interface with Korean text, and image generation. While acknowledging that some AI features are not exclusive to Samsung, he points out the S24's flatter edges, slightly bigger batteries, and functionally similar screen resolutions and ratios to the S23. The speaker humorously suggests the clickier buttons might be a notable difference, concluding that the phones are 'all grown up.' The segment concludes with a reflection on the progression from plastic to glass designs, referencing a black Samsung Galaxy S6 with an abstract wallpaper as a marker of past eras.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a sleek, silver Samsung Galaxy S23 smartphone, standing upright on a wooden block against a blue backdrop, emphasizing its triple-camera module and minimalist design. The scene transitions to the Man in Black Shirt, seated in a modern, well-lit room with a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape. He gestures expressively while holding a black Samsung smartphone, comparing it with a white Samsung smartphone, highlighting design differences, and noting how the 'Ultra' has been battling the 'Note,' winning, and even 'stole the S-Pen from it.' He then refers to 'boring default base phones for 750 bucks' as 'totally fine' but encourages viewers to consider more expensive options. A dynamic title card appears, displaying '2023 GALAXY S23' in bold blue text, surrounded by office supplies and a Rubik’s cube, reinforcing the phone’s modern appeal. The Man in Black Shirt then reveals the 'Galaxy S23 was interesting for one reason and one reason only: no more Exynos version.' He clarifies that every single Galaxy S23, S23 Plus, and S23 Ultra now ships with the 'same slightly customized Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 for Galaxy.' He demonstrates the phone’s functionality by showing its home screen, clock app, and Google Assistant interface, including voice commands for weather updates. He continues to engage the audience with animated hand gestures, emphasizing key features while holding the white phone, concluding that 'performance is great' because 'everyone now gets the good Snapdragon performance.' The segment ends with a close-up of the phone’s screen displaying the time, weather, and app icons, followed by a demonstration of Google Assistant responding to a voice query about the weather.

The video opens with a close-up of a hand holding a white Samsung smartphone, displaying its home screen with app icons and the time '10:03 AM' on Tuesday, February 27. The user navigates through the phone, opening the Messages app, which shows a welcome screen, and then proceeds to the Samsung Pay app, where a permission request appears asking to allow phone calls. The scene transitions to the Man in Black Shirt, seated in a modern room with a large monitor displaying a scenic landscape in the background. He holds the white smartphone and speaks directly to the camera, using expressive hand gestures, explaining that there's "less competition" for Qualcomm, leading to "less incentive" for innovation, despite the original goal for Exynos chips being "better customization and integration." He notes that while some aspects were removed, they can still claim "some customization" with the S23, having made it a "for Galaxy chip" rather than an "off the shelf one." He then asks, "What's different?" and elaborates on "slightly higher clock speeds, slightly more powerful 'cause they've got better cooling in these phones," concluding, "mission accomplished." He rotates the phone to show its triple-camera module and sleek design, continuing to explain that they've "removed even more character by getting rid of the signature camera bump and making an even more generic and universal appeal phone than ever before," featuring the "same set of cameras, same aspect ratio, same features, just slightly more battery in both versions." The video then cuts to a series of cinematic close-ups of the white smartphone lying on a dark surface, showcasing its camera array, metallic edges, and overall build quality under dramatic lighting, with a front view displaying the time '11:54 AM' on Wednesday, February 14. The segment concludes with the Man in Black Shirt continuing his presentation, holding the phone and gesturing as he speaks, emphasizing that "Nobody's saying these phones are bad, they're just not making exciting strides the way they" as the modern room and monitor remain in the background.

The video opens with a top-down flat-lay shot of a white Samsung Galaxy S23 smartphone, then transitions to a similar setup showing the 2024 Galaxy S24, surrounded by tech-themed items. The Man in Black Shirt, the primary speaker, introduces the S24, noting its 'even more boring colors and even more generic design and even more playing it safe than ever before.' The scene cuts to a black Samsung smartphone displaying a home screen, then a hand uses a stylus to interact with a smartphone, drawing a box around an image and showing a call interface with Korean text, visually demonstrating the S24's 'Snapdragon 8 Gen 3' chip and 'pretty substantial AI features' like 'live translation' and 'Circle to Search.' He then discusses the S24's physical design, stating that 'aside from just getting flatter edges and slightly bigger batteries,' it 'share[s] a lot of parts with the previous year's phones, like the cameras,' and that 'screen resolutions' and 'screen to body ratios' are 'functionally the same.' The Man in Black Shirt is then seen holding and comparing a white and a black smartphone, making a distinct clicking sound as he humorously suggests, 'Maybe the buttons are clickier. Maybe that's enough,' concluding that 'These phones are all grown up now.' The video concludes with a close-up of a black Samsung Galaxy S6 on a wooden surface, displaying a purple abstract wallpaper, as the speaker reflects on the evolution of phones, stating, 'So, now we've seen the glow up, the different eras, the plastic to the glass, the iconic...'

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Gray Hoodie, seated in a modern, well-lit room, discusses Samsung Galaxy base models, starting with screen sizes which evolved from 4 inches on the S1 to 6.8 inches on the S24, with a period of settling around 6 inches before the Ultra models introduced larger displays. He then moves to battery capacity, noting a significant increase from 1500 mAh on the S1 to 4000 mAh on the S20 and subsequent models, despite battery life not dramatically improving. The main camera megapixel count saw a dramatic rise from 5 MP on the S1 to 50 MP on the S22, S23, and S24, with a period of fluctuation and a plateau deemed appropriate as most screens do not match such high resolutions. Finally, the starting price has climbed from $399 for the S1 to $799 for the S24, with the introduction of Ultra models allowing for higher price ceilings. The video also briefly shows a black Samsung smartphone, possibly a Galaxy S23 or S24, on a wooden surface and later on a patterned surface with a red box labeled 'Marion Bataille' and a red Android figurine, demonstrating its placement on a black spherical charging dock. The Man in Gray Hoodie also mentions Dbrand as a sponsor, highlighting their cases' built-in magnets for Qi2/MagSafe compatibility on the S24, and concludes by remarking on the significant advancements in these phones.

The accompanying male voice describes this as a 'classic improvement over time,' noting the early drive to 'level up' for video resolution (720p to 4K) and how the need for 'too crazy' megapixels diminished after 8K, contrasting the baseline phone's trend with ultra-high-end models reaching 100-200 MP.

The male speaker highlights the 'steep climb' in prices, identifying a '$900 ceiling' for the base models and noting that 'Ultra' versions have pushed prices even higher.

The segment opens with a Man in Gray Hoodie, seated in a modern, well-lit room, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He wears a gray hoodie with a red inner collar and a smartwatch on his left wrist. Behind him, a large monitor displays a vibrant landscape, and his desk holds various tech items. He discusses the 'ups and downs' in progress graphs, noting that while it 'feels like they're going backwards,' there are 'reasons behind all of it.' He explains that he's focusing on 'just the base models' for 'pure simplicity of visuals,' despite the existence of 'Edge models, Plus models, Ultra models, the Active models.' As he says, 'First up, screen sizes,' the scene transitions to a detailed animated bar chart titled 'Screen Size [INCHES]'. This chart visually tracks the evolution of Samsung Galaxy S series screen sizes from the S1 (2010) to the S24 (2024). He narrates the chart's progression, starting with the 'original 4-inch Samsung Galaxy S,' then 'really quickly jumping up into the fives,' and 'slowing down and settling in right around 6 inches.' He highlights a 'slight dip' where the S10 was 6.1 inches, then 6.2 again, then back to 6.1 'to make room for the Ultra,' before returning to 6.2 inches. The chart dynamically populates with specific screen dimensions for each model year, including 4.0, 4.3, 4.8, 5.0, 5.1, 5.8, 6.1, and 6.2 inches, culminating in 6.8 inches for the S24. Key models like the S10 (2019) and S20 (2020) are emphasized with white bars. He concludes by observing that 'now there's a sort of a dedicated gigantic phone,' referring to the Ultra at '6.8 inches,' and that 'this rate of growth has pretty much evened out.'

The video opens with an animated blue bar chart titled 'Screen Size [INCHES]' on a lavender background, illustrating the evolution of Samsung Galaxy S series screen sizes from 4.0 inches (S1, 2010) to 6.8 inches (S24, 2024). Key models like S10 and S20 are highlighted with white bars, as a male speaker notes that the visually emphasized Galaxy S20 represented 'peak Samsung' because its screen size matches today's base phone. The visual then smoothly transitions to a green-themed 'Battery [mAh]' chart on a light green background, animating the progression of battery capacity. The speaker elaborates on 'battery sizes,' explaining that while battery life hasn't dramatically improved, the physical capacity has significantly increased, starting at 1500 mAh (removable) in 2010, nearly doubling by the Galaxy S5, then dipping when edge phones were introduced, before steadily climbing back up to 4000 mAh. The S20, again highlighted with a white bar, is identified as the first model with a 4000 mAh battery, reinforcing its 'peak Samsung' status. Finally, the video shifts to a red-themed 'Main Camera [MEGA PIXELS]' chart on a soft pink background, which remains static, emphasizing the dramatic increase in camera resolution from 5 MP in 2010 to 200 MP in 2024, with the S24 model highlighted in white, as the speaker introduces the topic of 'the primary camera megapixel count.'

The segment opens with a red-themed animated bar chart titled 'Main Camera [MEGA PIXELS]' against a soft pink background, visually tracking the evolution of the main camera megapixels from the Samsung Galaxy S1 (2010) to the S24 (2024). Vertical bars sequentially appear, displaying megapixel values from 5 MP for S1, rising to 8 MP for S2 and S3, 13 MP for S4, 16 MP for S5 and S6, then dropping to 12 MP for S7, S8, and S9, before rising again to 16 MP for S10, 12 MP for S20 and S21, and finally jumping to 50 MP for S22, S23, and S24. A male speaker provides commentary, noting a 'classic improvement over time' and explaining that while 'Megapixels don't mean everything,' it's interesting to see priorities increase, especially with the early incentive to 'level up' from 720p to 1080p to 4K video. He suggests that once 8K was hit, there wasn't much reason to go 'too crazy with megapixels' for baseline phones, contrasting this with 'highest-end ultra phones' that quickly blasted to 100 and 200 megapixels. He deems the 50 MP plateau 'appropriate' as 'most people's screens aren't 50 megapixels.' The scene then transitions to a purple-themed animated bar chart titled 'Starting Price [USD]' on a light lavender background, which the speaker introduces as 'last but not least, starting price.' This chart illustrates the starting price progression from S1 ($399 in 2010) to S24 ($799 in 2024), showing a 'steep climb' through $400, $500, $600, 'right up to the $900 ceiling' for the base Galaxy S models. The speaker clarifies this is for the 'base phone,' acknowledging that the 'most expensive phone has of course gotten dramatically more expensive too,' and that the 'ceiling lifting with the Ultra has allowed Samsung' to further increase prices.
